commands.description: List all pods in the current namespace with basic status, restarts count, and age.
commands.example:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get pods -o wide
commands.mistake: Looking for pods in the default namespace when they are deployed in a specific namespace. Use -n <namespace> or -A to list all namespaces.

commands.description: Show detailed state information of a specific pod, including lifecycle events, container states, and volumes.
commands.mistake: Running kubectl describe when logs would be more useful for application errors. Describe is best for scheduling errors, ImagePullBackOff, or Evicted states.

commands.description: Print the logs for a container in a pod. Useful for troubleshooting startup or application run errors.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to specify the container name using -c if the pod has multiple containers (e.g. sidecars or initContainers).

commands.description: Execute a command inside a running container of a pod. Often used to open an interactive bash/sh shell.
commands.mistake: Forgetting the double-dash '--' separator before the command, which causes kubectl to parse shell flags incorrectly.

commands.description: Set a new size for a deployment, replica set, or stateful set. Increases or decreases container instances.
commands.mistake: Manually scaling replicas for resources managed by a HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA), as HPA will overwrite your scaling adjustments immediately.

commands.description: Watch the progress of a deployment update (rolling restart/upgrade) until it finishes.
commands.mistake: Running next steps in CI/CD pipeline without checking rollout status, resulting in broken deployment failures going undetected.

commands.description: Roll back a deployment to the previous revision, or rollback to a specific revision history number.
commands.mistake: Rolling back without checking deployment rollout history first (via 'kubectl rollout history'), which might make you roll back to a bad state.

commands.description: Forward one or more local ports to a pod or service. Great for debugging cluster microservices locally without exposing them.
commands.mistake: Closing the terminal window or shell process while using port-forward, which terminates the active forwarding tunnel.

commands.description: Expose a deployment, pod, or replica set as a new Kubernetes Service, mapping internal ports to cluster IPs or load balancers.
commands.mistake: Exposing services with target ports that do not match the port exposed by the application in the container image.

commands.description: Display CPU and memory usage metrics for cluster nodes or pods. Requires Metrics Server to be installed in the cluster.
commands.example:
kubectl top pod -n production
kubectl top node
commands.mistake: Assuming top works without Metrics Server deployed, resulting in a 'Metrics API not available' error.

commands.description: Print connection and diagnostic endpoint URLs for cluster master and core addons services (DNS, dashboard).
commands.mistake: Running commands without checking cluster-info context, causing commands to run against production instead of staging/local.

commands.description: Create a new namespace within the cluster to isolate resource groups.
commands.mistake: Creating namespaces manually for production apps instead of using Infrastructure as Code (IaC) manifests, which leads to configuration drift.

commands.description: Change the default namespace for the current active kubectl context permanently, avoiding having to append -n every time.
commands.mistake: Forgetting which namespace is set as default and accidentally deleting or modifying resources in the wrong namespace.

commands.description: Create a ConfigMap from literal values, files, or directories to inject non-sensitive configuration data into containers.
commands.mistake: Storing sensitive information like database passwords or API keys in a ConfigMap instead of using a Kubernetes Secret resource.

commands.description: Create a Secret resource containing sensitive information (e.g. passwords, OAuth tokens, SSH keys). Automatically Base64 encodes values.
commands.mistake: Committing raw cleartext passwords to Git repositories inside shell commands or files used to create secrets.

commands.description: Copy files and directories to or from containers in a running pod. Great for pulling log files or uploading hotfixes during debug sessions.
commands.example:
kubectl cp ./nginx.conf web-pod-abc-123:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
kubectl cp web-pod-abc-123:/var/log/nginx/error.log ./error.log
commands.mistake: Using 'kubectl cp' and forgetting that the container requires the 'tar' binary installed inside it for file copies to work.

commands.description: Create and run a temporary, interactive pod in the cluster. Highly useful for running network diagnostic tools like ping, curl, or nslookup.
commands.mistake: Omitting the '--rm' flag, which leaves the debugging pod in Completed/Error state in your cluster forever after you exit.

commands.description: Create or apply configuration changes to resources from a file or stdin using declarative YAML/JSON files.
commands.example:
kubectl apply -f deployments/web-app.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s-configs/
commands.mistake: Running 'kubectl apply' without first validating configurations with 'kubectl diff' or '--dry-run=client' to verify changes.

commands.description: Delete Kubernetes resources by file path, resource type and name, or label selectors.
commands.example:
kubectl delete -f deployments/web-app.yaml
kubectl delete pod -l app=nginx
commands.mistake: Using 'kubectl delete' on resources without checking dependencies or target namespaces, accidentally taking down critical system resources.

commands.description: List events in the current namespace sorted by creation timestamp. Crucial for understanding scheduling failures, crashes, and volume issues.
commands.mistake: Filtering through massive lists of events without sorting or restricting to a namespace, making it hard to find relevant error events.
